problem.summary: SHA-256 hotspot sha256_block_data_order_riscv64 runs entirely in scalar on SG2044 (RVV 1.0 @ VLEN=128), with zero RVV vector instructions. Each 32-bit word requires 11 scalar instructions for byte load and endianness conversion, forming a severe performance bottleneck. test_evp duration: 210s, IPC: 1.881 (compute-bound).
evidence: perf annotate: sha256_block_data_order_riscv64 (3388 samples) shows zero v* vector instructions; all lbu/lw/sw/addw/sllw/srlw/or/xor/and scalar instructions
perf stat: IPC=1.881, L1_dcache_load_miss_rate=0.231%, confirming compute-bound (not memory-bound)
perf stat: duration_s=210.14, test_evp massive hotspot cost
SG2044 hardware explicitly supports RVV 1.0 (VLEN=128) but SHA-256 code does not use vector instructions
OpenSSL 3.x has existing RVV acceleration paths: sha256-riscv64-zvkb-zvknha_or_zvknhb.S in crypto/sha/asm/

rootCause.summary: OpenSSL fips.so was built without the 'v' extension (compiled as rv64gc scalar-only), leaving SG2044's RVV 1.0 vector hardware completely unused. The linux64-riscv64 target in Configurations/10-main.conf does not set -march=rv64gcv.
performanceFactors: IPC=1.881 (scalar typical, confirms no vector width utilization)
11 scalar instructions per 32-bit word for byte load + endianness conversion
At VLEN=128 with m1, single vle32.v could load 4 words

mustPreserve: Backward compatibility: systems without 'v' extension must continue to work via runtime scalar fallback
FIPS module integrity: fips.so must pass FIPS self-tests after rebuild
Existing public API: no changes to OpenSSL API surface
Cross-compilation: change must not break non-RISC-V targets
Runtime dispatch: crypto/riscvcap.c riscv_hwprobe() must correctly select vector vs scalar paths
